articles.description: Ishan Kishan has become the new world number one T20I batter, surpassing teammate Abhishek Sharma in the latest ICC rankings. Kishan's consistent form in the T20 World Cup propelled him to the top spot, making him the fourth Indian male player to do so.

articles.title: Watch: Australian man goes to Thailand hotel with minor, comes out with her body in suitcase; caught at airport
articles.description: An Australian man, Simon Peter Carman, has been charged with the murder of a 17-year-old Thai girl in Pattaya. Her body was discovered in a suitcase near a railway line. CCTV footage reportedly shows Carman leaving a condominium with the suitcase before abandoning it. He was arrested attempting to flee the country and denies all charges, claiming the girl became 'crazy' and threatened him.

articles.title: Union Government Moves To Evict Delhi Gymkhana Club, Cites Defence Use And 1928 Lease Deed | News18
articles.description: | The Union government has initiated proceedings to evict the Delhi Gymkhana Club from its 27.3-acre Safdarjung Road premises, stating that the land is required for defence infrastructure, public security, and other national purposes. The Centre has invoked the original 1928 lease deed while seeking possession of the property, marking a significant development in a long-standing land dispute. The move is expected to spark legal scrutiny and debate over heritage property use, club privileges, and government land requirements in central Delhi. n18oc_breaking-news News18 Mobile App - https://onelink.to/desc-youtube

articles.title: Delhi May Soon Get Its Own London Eye As DDA Revives Giant Observation Wheel Project
articles.description: Delhi could soon have its own iconic observation wheel with the proposed Delhi Eye project in Mayur Vihar. Planned by the Delhi Development Authority (DDA), the 35-metre-tall attraction aims to become a major tourism and recreational landmark in East Delhi. The project will be developed through a public-private partnership, with private firms invited to build, operate and maintain the facility while the land remains with the DDA. If completed, the Delhi Eye could offer panoramic views of the capital and transform Mayur Vihar into a new tourism hotspot.

articles.title: Beyond surveillance: humanitarian lessons on integrating physical health into refugee health responses
articles.description: Agreement: I Agree Body: Dear Editor, The BMJ article by Touhid powerfully illustrates how dengue exposes the fragility of health systems serving displaced populations. While the immediate challenge is vector-borne disease, the wider lesson extends beyond Bangladesh. Humanitarian responses must resist separating infectious disease control from broader physical healthcare and communication support. During the UK Afghan resettlement programme following the fall of Kabul in 2021, we observed a similar principle in a very different setting. Although our role primarily focused on mental health and safeguarding assessments, many newly arrived families presented with untreated physical health needs that had remained unrecognised throughout displacement, including poorly controlled chronic diseases, dental pathology, anaemia, pregnancy-related complications, and unmet disability needs. These conditions were not unusual; they reflected disrupted healthcare access and the realities of forced migration. The Bangladesh experience highlights how infectious diseases flourish where health systems are overwhelmed, surveillance is incomplete, and displaced communities face barriers to care. Refugee camps in Cox's Bazar present obvious environmental risks for dengue transmission, but the exclusion of camp data from national surveillance also demonstrates how health inequalities can become statistically invisible. Similar invisibility occurs in high-income countries when refugee health is viewed solely through a mental health or safeguarding lens, while physical health needs rely on individuals successfully navigating unfamiliar healthcare systems. Communication is central to both contexts. Early recognition of deteriorating illness—whether dengue shock syndrome or pregnancy complications—depends on timely access to healthcare, professional interpretation, culturally appropriate health information, and trust in health services. These are not optional additions but core patient safety interventions. Delays in seeking care should not be understood simply as individual decisions but as consequences of structural, linguistic, and systemic barriers. Humanitarian medicine has long recognised that physical health, mental health, and social circumstances are inseparable. Initial health assessments routinely incorporate infectious disease, chronic illness, nutrition, pregnancy, disability, and psychosocial needs within a single framework. As global displacement increases and climate change alters the epidemiology of infectious diseases, this integrated approach becomes increasingly relevant beyond humanitarian settings. The authors rightly emphasise the interaction between climate change, displacement, and dengue. Health systems must now prepare not only for emerging infectious diseases but also for increasingly mobile populations whose healthcare has often been interrupted long before arrival. Strengthening surveillance is essential, but so too is ensuring that displaced populations receive holistic, accessible, and communication-aware healthcare from first contact. Bangladesh's experience reminds us that the consequences of delayed recognition are measured not only in epidemiological data, but also in preventable suffering and avoidable deaths. Humanitarian health responses offer valuable lessons for health systems worldwide: comprehensive screening, equitable communication, and integrated physical and mental healthcare should be regarded as fundamental components of preparedness rather than emergency measures. No competing Interests: Yes The following competing Interests: Electronic Publication Date: Wednesday, July 1, 2026 - 05:44 AI use: Yes I have used AI Highwire Comment Subject: Dengue poses an increasingly severe threat to Bangladesh—and the world’s largest refugee camp AI use details: No AI tools were used to generate the content, ideas, analysis, or authorship of this article. AI-assisted software was used solely for minor grammar and spelling checks. Tensions are escalating after a period of relative calm, with intermittent border clashes and air strikes claiming dozens of lives recently.

articles.title: Major automakers record strong June sales on steady domestic demand, rising exports
articles.description: Indian auto manufacturers celebrated robust June sales, fueled by strong domestic demand and surging exports. Maruti Suzuki reported over 200,000 units sold, while Mahindra & Mahindra saw a significant 37% growth with over 106,000 vehicles. Hyundai Motor India also posted strong figures, overcoming a temporary production setback due to a supplier issue. These impressive numbers highlight a thriving automotive sector.

articles.description: In case of a bounce-back, the Nifty 50 is likely to face resistance in the 24,000-24,100 zone. However, support is placed at 23,800, as a fall below this level could bring the bears to the forefront. Overall, the trading range for the index remains at 23,800-24,200 going ahead.
